**Ningbo**[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city") in northeastern [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"), [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China"). It comprises six urban districts, two [satellite county-level cities](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city"), and two rural counties, including several islands in [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay") and the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea"). Ningbo is the southern economic center[[3]](#cite_note-4) of the [Yangtze Delta megalopolis](/wiki/Yangtze_Delta "Yangtze Delta"), which includes [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") among other cities.[[4]](#cite_note-art_1229099770_3707060-5) The [port of Ningbo–Zhoushan](/wiki/Port_of_Ningbo%E2%80%93Zhoushan "Port of Ningbo–Zhoushan"), spread across several locations, is the world's [busiest port by cargo tonnage](/wiki/List_of_busiest_ports_by_cargo_tonnage "List of busiest ports by cargo tonnage") and the world's third-[busiest container port](/wiki/List_of_busiest_container_ports "List of busiest container ports") since 2010.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Ningbo is the core city and center of the [Ningbo Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Ningbo_Metropolitan_Area "Ningbo Metropolitan Area").[[4]](#cite_note-art_1229099770_3707060-5) To the north, [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay") separates Ningbo from [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); to the east lies [Zhoushan](/wiki/Zhoushan "Zhoushan") in the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea"); on the west and south, Ningbo borders [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing") and [Taizhou](/wiki/Taizhou,_Zhejiang "Taizhou, Zhejiang") respectively. As of the [2020 Chinese national census](/wiki/Seventh_National_Population_Census_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Seventh National Population Census of the People's Republic of China"), the entire administrated area of Ningbo City had a population of 9.4 million (9,404,283).[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Ningbo is one of the 15 [sub-provincial cities](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city") in China, and is one of the five [separate state-planning cities](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%AE%A1%E5%88%92%E5%8D%95%E5%88%97%E5%B8%82 "zh:计划单列市")[[7]](#cite_note-8) in China (the other four being [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian"), [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), and [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen")), with the municipality possessing a separate state-planning status in many economic departments, rather than being governed by [Zhejiang Province](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"). Therefore, Ningbo has provincial-level autonomy in making economic and financial policies.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

In 2022, the [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of Ningbo was CNY 1570,43 billion[[9]](#cite_note-10) (US$233.479 billion), and it was ranked 12th among 293 cities in China.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Moreover, Ningbo is among the wealthiest cities in China; it ranked 8th in terms of average yearly disposable income in the year of 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-12) As of 2020, Ningbo has global headquarters and registered offices of over 100 listed companies,[[12]](#cite_note-13) and many regional business headquarters. In 2021, Ningbo featured the seventh most listed companies of all cities in China.[[13]](#cite_note-14) Furthermore, Ningbo was among the top 10 Chinese cities in the Urban Business Environment Report released by the Chinese state media [China Central Television (CCTV)](/wiki/CCTV-1 "CCTV-1") in 2019.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

As a city with rich culture and a long history dating back to the [Jingtou Mountain Culture](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%95%E5%A4%B4%E5%B1%B1%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80 "zh:井头山遗址") in 6300 BCE and the [Hemudu culture](/wiki/Hemudu_culture "Hemudu culture") in 4800 BCE, Ningbo was awarded "City of Culture in East Asia" by the governments of China, Japan, and Korea in 2016.[[15]](#cite_note-16) From 1842, Ningbo was one of the first five [treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_port "Treaty port") opened up to the West. Ningbo is one of the top 100 cities in the world by scientific research as tracked by the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index").[[16]](#cite_note-:1-17)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The first character in the city's name *ning* ([宁](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AE%81#Simplified_Chinese "wikt:宁") or 寧) means "serene", while its second character *bo* ([波](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B3%A2#Chinese "wikt:波")) translates to "wave". The city is abbreviated "[甬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%94%AC#Chinese "wikt:甬")" ([pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Yǒng*) for the eponymous "Yong Hill" (甬山), a prominent coastal hill near the city, and the [Yong River](/wiki/Yong_River_(Zhejiang) "Yong River (Zhejiang)") that flows through Ningbo city.

Formerly known as Mingzhou (明州; Míngzhōu), Ningbo boasts a rich historical background. The name Mingzhou is derived from the characters "[明](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%98%8E#Chinese "wikt:明")" (Míng), which symbolizes the presence of two lakes within the city walls: the Sun Lake (日湖) and the [Moon Lake](/wiki/Moon_Lake_(Ningbo) "Moon Lake (Ningbo)") (月湖). This nomenclature traces its roots back to the Tang dynasty in 636 CE. While the original Sun Lake dried up during the 19th century, the Ningbo government embarked on its restoration in 2002.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Ningbo](/wiki/History_of_Ningbo "History of Ningbo")

Ningbo is one of China's oldest cities, with a history dating back to the [Jingtou Mountain Culture](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%95%E5%A4%B4%E5%B1%B1%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80 "zh:井头山遗址") in 6300 BCE and [Hemudu culture](/wiki/Hemudu_culture "Hemudu culture") in 4800 BCE. Ningbo was known as a trade city on the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") at least two thousand years ago, and later as a major port for foreign trade. According to [Erik Zürcher](/wiki/Erik_Z%C3%BCrcher "Erik Zürcher"), among the nineteen Ashokan stupas in China, those at Changgan Temple in Nanjing and Ayuwang (Ashoka) Temple near Ningbo were particularly venerated, highlighting their importance in the early spread of Buddhism in China.[[17]](#cite_note-ArsOrient-18)

### Ancient to Sui dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient to Sui dynasty")]

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

As of 2020, the earliest relics of human activity discovered in Ningbo City are from the [Jingtou Mountain site](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%95%E5%A4%B4%E5%B1%B1%E9%81%97%E5%9D%80 "zh:井头山遗址") in Yuyao.[[18]](#cite_note-Chinese_Archaeology-19) These relics date back to 6300 BCE, evidencing early human consumption of seafood and rice. A large number of cultivated rice, farming tools, remains of dry-fence buildings, remains of domestic livestock, and primitive religious items have been unearthed from related sites of the [Hemudu culture](/wiki/Hemudu_culture "Hemudu culture") (5000–4500 BCE), evidencing human settlement and culture in the eastern part of the Ningshao Plain, where modern-day Ningbo city is located.[[18]](#cite_note-Chinese_Archaeology-19)

Before the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), the area where Ningbo City is located today was sparsely populated. In the [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty"), the location of Ningbo was called "Yin". In the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period"), the area where Ningbo to which Ningbo belonged was the [Yue State](/wiki/Yue_(state) "Yue (state)"). At that time, the Yue King Goujian built Juzhang City in the present-day Cicheng Town, which became the earliest city in Ningbo. In the latter half of the [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") period, the area of Ningbo became the jurisdiction of [Chu State](/wiki/Chu_(state) "Chu (state)"). In 221 BCE, Qin unified the six states and the Ningbo area was delegated to [Kuaiji Commandery](/wiki/Kuaiji_Commandery "Kuaiji Commandery"), with three counties of Yin, Yin, and Juzhang (some studies assert there were four counties: Yin, Yin, Juzhang, and Yuyao). In the early years of the Western Han dynasty, [Kuaiji Commandery](/wiki/Kuaiji_Commandery "Kuaiji Commandery") belonged to the Kingdom of Jing and Wu. After the Seven Kingdoms was settled, [Kuaiji Commandery](/wiki/Kuaiji_Commandery "Kuaiji Commandery") was restored. In 589 CE (Sui Kai Huang nine years), the counties were merged under the Wu kingdom.

### Tang and Song dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tang and Song dynasty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Tianfeng_Tower.jpg/250px-Tianfeng_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tianfeng_Tower.jpg)

Tianfeng Tower, originally built during the Tang dynasty, is the symbol of old Ningbo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/A_rock_garden_inside_Tianyi_Chamber.JPG/250px-A_rock_garden_inside_Tianyi_Chamber.JPG)](/wiki/File:A_rock_garden_inside_Tianyi_Chamber.JPG)

A rock garden inside Tianyi Chamber.

Since the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), Ningbo has been an important commercial port. Arab traders lived in Ningbo during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") when it was known as Mingzhou or Siming,[[19]](#cite_note-Hagras_102-20) since the ocean-going trade passages took precedence over land trade during this time.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) It was a well known center of ocean-going commerce with the foreign world.[[22]](#cite_note-23) These merchants did not intermingle with native Chinese, instead practicing their own customs and religion and inhabiting ghettos. They did not try to proselytize Islam to the Chinese.[[23]](#cite_note-24) There was also a large Jewish community in Ningbo, as evidenced by the fact that, after a major flood destroyed [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") scrolls in [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng") in 1642, a replacement was sent to the [Kaifeng Jews](/wiki/Kaifeng_Jews "Kaifeng Jews") by the [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") at Ningbo.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

From the Tang to the late Ming dynasty, the ports of Ningbo and [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou") saw the most direct trade between China and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

### Ming dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ming dynasty")]

The city of Ningbo was known in Europe for a long time under the name of Liampó. This was the usual spelling used, for example in the standard Portuguese history, [João de Barros](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_de_Barros "João de Barros")'s *Décadas da Ásia*, although Barros explained that Liampó was a Portuguese "corruption" of the more correct Nimpó.[[25]](#cite_note-barros1.9.7-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) The spelling Liampó is also attested to in the *Peregrination* (*Peregrinação*) by [Fernão Mendes Pinto](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Mendes_Pinto "Fernão Mendes Pinto"), a (so-called) autobiography written in Portuguese during the 16th century. For the mid-16th-century Portuguese, the nearby promontory, which they called the cape of Liampó after the nearby "illustrious city", was the easternmost known point of the mainland Asia.[[25]](#cite_note-barros1.9.7-26) The Portuguese began trading in Ningbo around 1522. By 1542, the Portuguese had a sizable community in Ningbo (or, more likely, on nearby small islands such as [Shuangyu](/wiki/Shuangyu "Shuangyu")). Portuguese activities from their Ningbo base included pillaging and attacking multiple Chinese port cities around Ningbo. They also enslaved people during their raids.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Portuguese were ousted from the Ningbo area in 1548.

In May 1523, a brawl between trade representatives from rival Japanese clans escalated into pillaging and piracy, an event known as the [Ningbo incident](/wiki/Ningbo_incident "Ningbo incident").

### Qing dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Qing dynasty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Map_of_Ningbo_in_19th_century_.jpg/250px-Map_of_Ningbo_in_19th_century_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ningbo_in_19th_century_.jpg)

19th century map of Ningbo.[[19]](#cite_note-Hagras_102-20)

Ningbo was one of the five Chinese *[treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_ports "Treaty ports")* opened by the [Treaty of Nanjing](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanjing "Treaty of Nanjing") signed in 1842 at the end of the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") between the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and China. During the war, British forces briefly took possession of the walled city of Ningbo after storming the fortified town of [Zhenhai](/wiki/Zhenhai_District "Zhenhai District") at the mouth of the Yong River on October 10, 1841. The British subsequently repulsed a Chinese attempt to retake the city in the [Battle of Ningpo](/wiki/Battle_of_Ningpo "Battle of Ningpo") on 10 March 1842. In 1861, the forces of the [Taiping Kingdom](/wiki/Taiping_Kingdom "Taiping Kingdom") took the city relatively unopposed as the defending garrison and all Ningbo residents fled except for the [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") and [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"); they held the town for six months. In March 1885, during the [Sino-French War](/wiki/Sino-French_War "Sino-French War"), Admiral Courbet's naval squadron blockaded several Chinese warships in Zhenhai Bay and exchanged fire with the shore defenses.

Ningbo was also once famed for traditional Chinese furniture production, and western encyclopedias described Ningbo as a center of craftsmanship and industry.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

During the 19th century Ningbo authorities contracted Cantonese pirates to exterminate Portuguese pirates who had raided Canton shipping around Ningbo. The massacre was "successful", with 40 Portuguese dead and only 2 Cantonese dead. It was dubbed "[The Ningpo Massacre](/wiki/The_Ningpo_Massacre "The Ningpo Massacre")" by an English correspondent, who noted that the Portuguese pirates had behaved savagely towards the Cantonese Chinese, and that the Portuguese authorities at Macau should have reined in the pirates.

During the late Qing era, Western missionaries set up a [Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") in Ningbo. Li Veng-eing was a Reverend of the Ningpo Church.[[30]](#cite_note-31) The Ningpo College was managed by Rev. Robert F. Fitch. The four trustees were natives of Ningbo, and three of them had Taotai rank.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Rev. George Evans Moule, B.A., was appointed as a missionary to China by the [Church of England Missionary Society](/wiki/Church_of_England_Missionary_Society "Church of England Missionary Society"), and arrived at Ningpo with Mrs. Moule in February 1858. His time was chiefly divided between Ningpo and another mission station he began at Hang-chow. He wrote Christian publications in the [Ningbo dialect](/wiki/Ningbo_dialect "Ningbo dialect").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War II")]

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in 1940, between 80% and 90% of Ningbo's population fled Ningbo, leaving primarily the elderly behind.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The Japanese bombed Ningbo with ceramic bombs full of [fleas](/wiki/Flea "Flea") carrying [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague"). An outbreak of bubonic plague followed. Bacteriologist Huang Ketai reported that at least 109 people died from the plague in Ningbo in November and December 1940.[[34]](#cite_note-plague-35) According to Daniel Barenblatt, imperial planes loading germ bombs for bubonic dissemination over Ningbo were recorded on film in 1940.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Txu-oclc-10552568-nh51-10.jpg/250px-Txu-oclc-10552568-nh51-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Txu-oclc-10552568-nh51-10.jpg)

Ningpo (labeled YIN-HSIEN (NINGPO) 鄞縣) (1952)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Ninghai_Town_God_Temple%2C_2017-05-29_03.jpg/250px-Ninghai_Town_God_Temple%2C_2017-05-29_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ninghai_Town_God_Temple,_2017-05-29_03.jpg)

Ningbo city temple

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Cathedral_of_the_Sacred_Heart_of_Jesus_Ningbo.jpg/250px-Cathedral_of_the_Sacred_Heart_of_Jesus_Ningbo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_of_the_Sacred_Heart_of_Jesus_Ningbo.jpg)

The Cathedral of the Sacred Heart of Jesus was built between 1872 and 1876, was closed by the government in 1963, and was reopened and renamed in 1980. It was recognized as a national heritage site in 2006.

Ningbo ranges in latitude from 28° 51' to 30° 33' N and in longitude from 120° 55' to 122° 16' E, bounded on the east by the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea") and [Zhoushan Archipelago](/wiki/Zhoushan_Archipelago "Zhoushan Archipelago"); on the north by [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay"), across which it faces [Jiaxing](/wiki/Jiaxing "Jiaxing") and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); on the west by [Shaoxing](/wiki/Shaoxing "Shaoxing"); and on the south by [Taizhou](/wiki/Taizhou,_Zhejiang "Taizhou, Zhejiang"). Its land area is 9,816 square kilometers (3,790 sq mi), while its oceanic territory amounts to 9,758 km2 (3,768 sq mi); there is a total 1,562 km (971 mi) of coastline, including 788 km (490 mi) of mainland coastline and 774 km (481 mi) of island coastline that together accounting for one-third of the entire provincial coastline. There are 531 islands accounting for 524 km2 (202 sq mi) under the city's administration.

Ningbo's city proper is sandwiched between the ocean and low-lying mountains to the southwest, with coastal plain and valleys in between. Its central business district is bisected by the Yongjiang River. Important peninsulas include the [Chuanshan Peninsula](/w/index.php?title=Chuanshan_Peninsula&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chuanshan Peninsula (page does not exist)") (穿山半岛), located in Beilun District and containing mainland Zhejiang's easternmost point, and the [Xiangshan Peninsula](/w/index.php?title=Xiangshan_Peninsula&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xiangshan Peninsula (page does not exist)") (象山半岛) in [Xiangshan County](/wiki/Xiangshan_County,_Zhejiang "Xiangshan County, Zhejiang"). The [Siming Mountains](/w/index.php?title=Siming_Mountains&action=edit&redlink=1 "Siming Mountains (page does not exist)") (四明山) run north from [Mount Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai_Mountain "Tiantai Mountain") and within Ningbo City, traversing [Yuyao City](/wiki/Yuyao "Yuyao"), [Haishu District](/wiki/Haishu,_Ningbo "Haishu, Ningbo"), and [Fenghua District](/wiki/Fenghua,_Ningbo "Fenghua, Ningbo"), and reaching a height of 979 m (3,212 ft).

Tidal flat ecosystems occur adjacent to the city, however, large areas have been reclaimed for agricultural purposes.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Ningbo has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*) with four distinctive seasons, characterized by hot, humid summers and chilly, cloudy and dry winters (with occasional snow). The mean annual temperature is 17.6 °C (63.7 °F), with monthly daily averages ranging from 5.8 °C (42.4 °F) in January to 29.1 °C (84.4 °F) in July. Extremes since 1951 have ranged from −8.8 °C (16 °F) on 12 January 1955 to 42.1 °C (108 °F) on 8 August 2013.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The city receives an average annual rainfall of 1,430 mm (56 in) and is affected by the [plum rains](/wiki/Meiyu "Meiyu") of the Asian monsoon in June, when average relative humidity also peaks. From August to October, Ningbo experiences the effects of [typhoons](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon"), and is affected by an average 1.8 storms annually, though the city is not often struck directly by these systems. A 2012 OECD study lists Ningbo among the top 20 cities worldwide most at risk of flooding due to anthropogenic climate change.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

Ningbo is among the cities in China which are implementing [sponge city](/wiki/Sponge_city "Sponge city") strategies to mitigate the impacts of climate change.[[39]](#cite_note-:Curtis&Klaus-40)

## Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

**Local officers of Ningbo**

* The Secretary of Party in Ningbo is [Peng Jiaxue](/w/index.php?title=Peng_Jiaxue&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peng Jiaxue (page does not exist)"), who is first-in-charge of the city.
* The Mayor of Ningbo is [Qiu Dongyao](/w/index.php?title=Qiu_Dongyao_(politician,_born_1961)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Qiu Dongyao (politician, born 1961) (page does not exist)"), who is second-in command of the city, and the Vice Secretary of Party in Ningbo.

**Administrative divisions of Ningbo**

The sub-provincial city of Ningbo is as whole an urban group with one central group, one northern group, and one southern group.

It has direct jurisdiction over the following:

* Six [districts](/wiki/District_of_China "District of China") (central group): Haishu District, Yinzhou District, Jiangbei District, Beilun District, Zhenhai District, Fenghua District
* Two [county-level cities](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city") (northern group): Yuyao, Cixi
* Two [counties](/wiki/County_(People%27s_Republic_of_China) "County (People's Republic of China)") (southern group): Xiangshan, Ninghai

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

Ningbo is an important port city located 220 kilometers (140 mi) south of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). The city's export industry dates back to the 7th century. Today, Ningbo is a major exporter of electrical products, textiles, food, and industrial tools. The city's private sector is especially well-developed, contributing 80 percent of total GDP in 2013.[[50]](#cite_note-Ningbo_2014-51)

Historically, Ningbo was somewhat geographically isolated from other major cities. In 2007 the [Hangzhou Bay Bridge](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay_Bridge "Hangzhou Bay Bridge") was built, cutting highway transit time between Ningbo and Shanghai from four hours to two and a half. The city now serves as the economic center for the southern Yangtze River Delta and has been ranked among the most competitive cities in China.[[50]](#cite_note-Ningbo_2014-51)

In 2009, Ningbo's economic activity reached US$60.8 billion, down 10.4 percent from 2008. The exports totaled US$38.65 billion, down 16.6 percent from the previous year. In addition, Ningbo imported US$22.16 billion of goods, up 3.1 percent from the previous year.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Ningbo's economy grew 9.26 percent in 2013 to 712.89 billion yuan (US$115.12 billion).[[50]](#cite_note-Ningbo_2014-51) In 2009, the city's per capita output was US$10,833, about three times the national average.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

### Foreign investment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Foreign investment")]

With several important development zones established in or around Ningbo, the city has received considerable foreign investment.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Over 60 domestic and foreign-invested financial institutions have established operations in the city, which has also attracted more than 10,000 foreigners. The municipal government offers preferential policies designed to encourage investment in international trade, new strategic industries, manufacturing, information services, and creative industries.[[50]](#cite_note-Ningbo_2014-51)

### Economic and technological development zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economic and technological development zones")]

#### Ningbo Economic & Technological Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ningbo Economic & Technological Development Zone")]

Located in the north-east of Ningbo, behind Beilun Port, NETD is 27 km (17 mi) away from the city center. With more than 20 years of great effort, NETD has already formed the general framework for large scale construction and development, and established the perfect investment environment. It is situated close to the Ningbo Port and Ningbo Lishe International Airport. Major Investors include [ExxonMobil](/wiki/ExxonMobil "ExxonMobil"), [Dupont](/wiki/DuPont "DuPont") and [Dow Chemical](/wiki/Dow_Chemical_Company "Dow Chemical Company").[[54]](#cite_note-RightSite.asia-55)

#### Ningbo Daxie Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ningbo Daxie Development Zone")]

The Ningbo Daxie Development Zone was approved in 1993 and covers an area of 5.92 km2 (2.29 sq mi). Over more than ten years of development and construction, industrial and logistical foundations have been established in the zone for the transshipment of energy, liquid chemicals and containers.[[54]](#cite_note-RightSite.asia-55)

#### Ningbo National Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ningbo National Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone")]

Ningbo National Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone was founded in 1999 and was upgraded to a national level zone in January 2007. It is 10 km (6.2 mi) from Ningbo International Airport and 18 km (11 mi) away from Ningbo Port. The zone serves as the important technical innovation base of Yangtze River Delta. Industries encouraged include chemicals production and processing, biotechnology and pharmaceuticals, raw material processing, Research and Development.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

#### Ningbo Free Trade Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ningbo Free Trade Zone")]

Ningbo Free Trade Zone is one of the 15 free trade zones authorized by the State Council of China and is the only free trade zone in Zhejiang Province. It was established by State Council in 1992, covering the area of 2.3 km2 (0.89 sq mi). It lies in the middle of the coastline of mainland China, at the south of Yangtze River Delta. In 2008, its industrial output value was RMB 53.33 billion and grew at 19.8 percent as compared to 2007.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

#### Nordic Industrial Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Nordic Industrial Park")]

The Nordic Industrial Park Co. Ltd. (NIP) is one of the first wholly foreign-owned industrial parks in China located in Ningbo, Zhejiang Province. NIP is managed and operated by a Scandinavian management team.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

#### Ningbo Advertising Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ningbo Advertising Park")]

The Ningbo Advertising Park is a national level pilot park located in the Ningbo Southern Business District. The financial incentives have attracted over 300 relevant firms to establish operations.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

## Ningbo port

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ningbo port")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Beilun_Port_2020-05-02.jpg/250px-Beilun_Port_2020-05-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beilun_Port_2020-05-02.jpg)

Ningbo port in the Beilun District

Main article: [Port of Ningbo](/wiki/Port_of_Ningbo "Port of Ningbo")

Unlike other Chinese cities, Ningbo has the same authority as provincial governments for economic administration and is the largest port in the world in terms of annual cargo throughput. In contrast to Shanghai, the port is deepwater and capable of handling 300,000-ton vessels. The port is located mainly in Beilun District and Zhenhai District.

In 2006, Ningbo Port started its expansion towards the neighboring island city of [Zhoushan](/wiki/Zhoushan "Zhoushan") to build an even larger port with higher capacity to compete with neighboring ports in the region, such as [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai")'s Yangshan a deepwater port. Statistics in 2010 showed that total cargo throughput was 627,000,000 tons and container throughput was 13,144,000 TEUs. In 2021, total cargo throughput was 1,224,050,000 tons, including 31,080,000 TEUs. Ningbo proper saw 623,400,000 tons and 29,370,000 TEUs, while Zhoushan saw 600,650,000 tons and 1,710 000 TEUs.

Thus, with bulk container breakdowns, hugely improved logistics, and massive chemical and foodstuff processing developments, Ningbo is outcompeting Shanghai for the preeminent Chinese east coast port.[[59]](#cite_note-china-briefing.com-60)[[60]](#cite_note-2021年12月全国港口货物、集装箱吞吐量-政府信息公开-交通运输部-61)

Ningbo is part of the [21st Century Maritime Silk Road](/wiki/21st_Century_Maritime_Silk_Road "21st Century Maritime Silk Road") that runs from the Chinese coast to the south, via [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") towards the southern tip of [India](/wiki/India "India"), via [Mombasa](/wiki/Mombasa "Mombasa") to the Mediterranean, and from there via [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") to the Upper Adriatic region, to the northern Italian hub of [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), with its rail connections to [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Tianyi_Square_2019-06-09_01.jpg/330px-Tianyi_Square_2019-06-09_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tianyi_Square_2019-06-09_01.jpg)

Haishu District, Tianyi Square

Due to its long history and economic prosperity, Ningbo is a city with very rich tourist resources. The following is a list of the main tourist attractions authorized by the [Ningbo Municipal Bureau of Culture, Radio, Television and Tourism](http://wglyj.ningbo.gov.cn/col/col1229057431/index.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20210519015627/http://wglyj.ningbo.gov.cn/col/col1229057431/index.html) 2021-05-19 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") in each subdivision of Ningbo city.

### Haishu District

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Haishu District")]

* [Moon Lake](/wiki/Moon_Lake_(Ningbo) "Moon Lake (Ningbo)") Park (Yuehu Park): A [reservoir](/wiki/Man-made_lake "Man-made lake") excavated in the Tang dynasty (636 CE) at the center of Haishu District. The park includes the lake itself, some small islands on the lake, and many ancient Chinese architectures and historical sites, such as:
  + [Tianyi Pavilion](/wiki/Tianyi_Pavilion "Tianyi Pavilion") (or Tianyi Chamber): One of Ningbo's most popular and famous tourist attractions. Built in 1516 CE, it is the oldest library existent in Asia and is one of the 3 oldest private libraries in the world. The collection dates back to the 11th century and includes woodblock and handwritten copies of the [Confucian classics](/wiki/Confucian_classics "Confucian classics"), rare local histories, and lists of the candidates successful in [imperial examinations](/wiki/Imperial_examinations "Imperial examinations"). The currently Tianyi Pavilion refers to the whole museum complex that includes:
    - Tianyi Pavilion-related buildings such as:
    - The ancient Chinese mansion of the library's first owner, Fan Qing, built during the Ming dynasty (1516 CE)
    - A traditional [Chinese Garden](/wiki/Chinese_garden "Chinese garden") as part of Fan's mansion
    - An ancient private theater of Chinese plays as part of Fan's mansion
    - A [Mahjong](/wiki/Mahjong "Mahjong") (麻將) museum, since Ningbo is regarded as the birthplace of Mahjong
  + He Ancestral Temple (賀祕監祠): A cultural built in honor of the Chinese poet [He Zhizhang](/wiki/He_Zhizhang "He Zhizhang") (賀知章, 659 CE – 744 CE) during the Tang dynasty who called himself the "Siming Crazy Guest", where "Siming" is the name of a mountain in Ningbo. The building was completed during the Song dynasty (1144 CE) and repaired during the Qing dynasty (1865 CE).
  + [Ancient Korean Embassy](/w/index.php?title=Ancient_Korean_Embassy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ancient Korean Embassy (page does not exist)"): Built during the Northern Song dynasty to welcome Korean envoys and business groups. Destroyed by war in 1130 CE, the site was announced as a cultural relics protection unit in 1984. It is now a showroom for the history of relations between Ningbo and Korea.
  + Central Lake Temple, Central Lake East Bridge, Zhenming Ridge, and Xuanmiao Temple: A Ningbo-born novelist [Qu You](/wiki/Qu_You "Qu You") wrote a fiction called *[Peony Dengji](/w/index.php?title=Peony_Dengji&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peony Dengji (page does not exist)")* (牡丹燈記, [Janpanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): [Botan Dōrō](/wiki/Botan_D%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Botan Dōrō") also known as 怪談牡丹燈籠 *Tales of the Peony Lantern*) (in the collection of *[Jiandeng Xinhua](/wiki/Jiandeng_Xinhua "Jiandeng Xinhua")*). It describes a love story between ghost and a man during [Fang Guozhen](/wiki/Fang_Guozhen "Fang Guozhen") period. The story took place at the Moon Lake. Japanese scholar Koyama Issei identified many of the locations, including Central Lake Temple, Central Lake East Bridge, Zhenming Ridge, and Xuanmiao temple, that would fit geographically and architecturally of the places mentioned in the story. The story was adapted as one of three [Kaidan](/wiki/Kaidan_(parapsychology) "Kaidan (parapsychology)") tales in Japan.
* Drum Tower Complex (Haishu Tower): The only remaining ruin of an old city gate tower constructed during the Tang dynasty. At the top, there is a six-meter-high Romanesque bell tower added in the Republic period. Around the base of the tower is a commercial area where all the buildings are reconstructed in the traditional style.
* [Chenghuang](/wiki/Chenghuang "Chenghuang") Temple (Ningbo County Temple): An ancient temple of the City God at commercial center in downtown Ningbo.
* [Tianfeng Pagoda](/w/index.php?title=Tianfeng_Pagoda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tianfeng Pagoda (page does not exist)"): This national cultural relics protection site is a typical Song-style loft-style brickwood structure tower unique to Jiangnan. This hexagonal building is a landmark and the tallest ancient structure in the city. It appears as a seven-story tower with another seven stories underground, and is renowned for its long history, architectural value, and ancient artifacts. The tower was first built in 695 CE during the Tang dynasty (618–907 CE).
* [Tianyi Square](/wiki/Tianyi_Square "Tianyi Square"): Located in the bustling old downtown of Ningbo City with the nationwide famous shopping complex, named after the Tianyi Ge (Chamber), the oldest private library in Asia.
* Nantang Old Street: An old commercial street by the river with many folk arts and crafts shops, souvenir stores, and small restaurants. Previously, Nantang Street was a place for local fairs and flea markets. In 2013, the street was renovated to become a tourist site.
* Liangzhu Cultural Park: A theme park dedicated to the story of the [Butterfly Lovers](/wiki/Butterfly_Lovers "Butterfly Lovers"), one of the four folklores in China. The Butterfly Lovers is reputed as the oriental version of *Romeo and Juliet*.
* Baiyun Manor: An ancient academy where [Huang Zongxi](/wiki/Huang_Zongxi "Huang Zongxi") (黃宗羲) gave lectures. Huang, whose style name is Taichong, was a distinguished thinker, writer, and historian of the late Ming and early Qing.
* [Tashan Weir](/wiki/Tashan_Weir "Tashan Weir"): An ancient dam erected during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").
* Ningbo Wulongtan Scenic Scenic Resort: Also called Five-dragon Pools Scenic Resort, it is one of the Ten New Sceneries in Ningbo, and a National AAAA rated scenic area.

### Yinzhou District

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Yinzhou District")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/South_Gate_of_Ningbo_Museum.jpg/250px-South_Gate_of_Ningbo_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_Gate_of_Ningbo_Museum.jpg)

Ningbo Museum

* [Ningbo Museum](/wiki/Ningbo_Museum "Ningbo Museum") (Yinzhou Museum, or Ningbo Historic Museum): A museum focused on Ningbo area history and traditional customs, considered the masterwork of [Wang Shu](/wiki/Wang_Shu "Wang Shu"), the first Chinese citizen to win the [Pritzker Architecture Prize](/wiki/Pritzker_Architecture_Prize "Pritzker Architecture Prize") in 2012. The Ningbo Museum is also the main filming location for the 2023 TV series adaptation of scifi author Liu Cixin's novel of the same name, [Three Bodies.](/wiki/The_Three-Body_Problem_(novel) "The Three-Body Problem (novel)")
* [Romon U-Park](/wiki/Romon_U-Park "Romon U-Park"): One of the largest urban indoor theme parks in the world.
* Ningbo Eastern New Town: A newly developed area of Ningbo City, with a well-designed CBD (including two 400m skyscrapers and other headquarters of many listed company and government offices), several museums, galleries, and shopping centers, including:
  + Ningbo Urban Planning Exhibition Center[[64]](#cite_note-65)
  + Ningbo Hankyu Commercial Complex: Opened in 2021, it is the first overseas outlet of Hankyu, the famous Japanese department store.
  + Ningbo New Library
* Yinzhou Park and Ningbo Southern CBD
* [Dongqian Lake](/wiki/Dongqian_Lake "Dongqian Lake"): The largest natural freshwater lake in [Zhejiang Province](/wiki/Zhejiang_Province "Zhejiang Province"). The earliest historical record of the lake dates back to the [West Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)"), and there are several natural sceneries and historical attractions around the lake:
  + Little Putuo: An island on the lake with several temples built during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") by a prime minister called [Shihao](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B2%E6%B5%A9 "zh:史浩").
  + Yuefei Temple: A temple built during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") in memory of [Yuefei](/wiki/Yue_Fei "Yue Fei").
  + Taogong Island: The place where [Fanli](/wiki/Fan_Li "Fan Li") and [Xishi](/wiki/Xi_Shi "Xi Shi") once lived according to folklore.
  + [Southern Song Dynasty Rock Carving Park](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%8B%E7%9F%B3%E5%88%BB%E5%85%AC%E5%9B%AD "zh:南宋石刻公园"): won the "National Cultural Relics Conservation Best Project Award" awarded by the China National Architecture Research Association and the China Cultural Relics Conservation Foundation
  + Fuquan Mountain: a mountain with a Chinese tea theme park
  + Ningbo Hanling Old Street: an ancient street with a history thousands of years. It was once called Hanling City. The 'city' here means bazaar.
* [Qita Temple](/wiki/Qita_Temple "Qita Temple"): A Zen Buddhist temple complex first consecrated during the Tang dynasty in the downtown area of Ningbo city.
* [Tianhou Temple](/wiki/Tianhou_Temple_(Ningbo) "Tianhou Temple (Ningbo)"): A former temple of [Mazu](/wiki/Mazu_(goddess) "Mazu (goddess)") as the "Empress of Heaven" once used by Fujianese merchants as their guild hall (*Qing'an Huiguan*). In the 19th century, it was accounted by [S. Wells Williams](/wiki/S._Wells_Williams "S. Wells Williams") as the most beautiful place in Ningbo and by [John Thomson](/wiki/John_Thomson_(photographer) "John Thomson (photographer)") as one of the most beautiful temples in China, but that structure was destroyed during the Chinese Civil War. It has been reconstructed with many of its original works of art, however, to form the [East Zhejiang Maritime Affairs and Folk Customs Museum](/wiki/East_Zhejiang_Maritime_Affairs_and_Folk_Customs_Museum "East Zhejiang Maritime Affairs and Folk Customs Museum").
* [Temple of King Ashoka](/wiki/Temple_of_King_Ashoka "Temple of King Ashoka"): a [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple") first established during the [Western Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)") (282 AD).
* [Tiantong Temple](/wiki/Tiantong_Temple "Tiantong Temple"): One of the "Five [Chan Buddhism](/wiki/Chan_Buddhism "Chan Buddhism") Temples". Tiantong Temple is the cradle of the Sōtō school (曹洞宗, *Sōtō-shū*) of [Japanese Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan").
* Ningbo Ocean World: An aquarium with an ocean theater
* Ningbo Youngor Zoo
* Ningbo Sakura Park (Zhongxing Sakura Park): A sakura park built to commemorate the friendship city between Ningbo City and Nagaoka City, Japan

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Zhaobaoshan_Bridge_in_Zhenhai_District.jpg/250px-Zhaobaoshan_Bridge_in_Zhenhai_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhaobaoshan_Bridge_in_Zhenhai_District.jpg)

Zhao bao shan Bridge, Zhenhai District, Ningbo

### Bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Bridge")]

The [Hangzhou Bay Bridge](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay_Bridge "Hangzhou Bay Bridge"), a combination [cable-stayed bridge](/wiki/Cable-stayed_bridge "Cable-stayed bridge") and causeway across Hangzhou Bay, opened to the public on 1 May 2008. This bridge connects the municipalities of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and Ningbo and is considered the longest trans-oceanic bridge in the world.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) It is the world's second-longest bridge, after the [Lake Pontchartrain Causeway](/wiki/Lake_Pontchartrain_Causeway "Lake Pontchartrain Causeway") in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), United States.

The Jintang Bridge, a four-lane sea crossing bridge linking Jintang Island of Zhoushan and Zhenhai district, is a 27 km (17 mi) long opened on December 26, 2009.

The [Xiangshan Harbor Bridge](/wiki/Xiangshan_Harbor_Bridge "Xiangshan Harbor Bridge") opened to traffic on December 29, 2012, connecting Ningbo with Xiangshan. The 47 km (29 mi) long project includes 22 km (14 mi) as the main body of the bridge and an 8 kilometer long tunnel.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

### Sea port

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Sea port")]

The port of Ningbo is the world's busiest port. It was ranked number 1 in total Cargo Volume (1.22 billion tons in 2021[[60]](#cite_note-2021年12月全国港口货物、集装箱吞吐量-政府信息公开-交通运输部-61)) and number 3 in total container traffic (31.1 million TEUs in 2021) since 2019.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Airport")]

[Ningbo Lishe International Airport](/wiki/Ningbo_Lishe_International_Airport "Ningbo Lishe International Airport") connects Ningbo by air to the rest of China, with regularly scheduled domestic and international flights.

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Ningbo_New_Railway_Station_Interior.JPG/250px-Ningbo_New_Railway_Station_Interior.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ningbo_New_Railway_Station_Interior.JPG)

Ningbo New Railway Station was reopened in December 2013 after years of on-site restoration to accommodate high speed rails and increasing passengers

Three railway lines intersect in Ningbo: the [Xiaoshan–Ningbo Railway](/wiki/Xiaoshan%E2%80%93Ningbo_Railway "Xiaoshan–Ningbo Railway") (Xiaoyong Line), which runs west to [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"); the [Ningbo–Taizhou–Wenzhou (Yongtaiwen) Railway](/wiki/Ningbo%E2%80%93Taizhou%E2%80%93Wenzhou_Railway "Ningbo–Taizhou–Wenzhou Railway"), which runs south to [Wenzhou](/wiki/Wenzhou "Wenzhou"); and the [Hangzhou–Ningbo High-Speed Railway](/wiki/Hangzhou%E2%80%93Ningbo_High-Speed_Railway "Hangzhou–Ningbo High-Speed Railway"), which runs parallel to the Xiaoyong Line providing high-speed railway service.

With the booming economy in the region, the Xiaoyong Railway, a conventional railway built in the 1950s, could not meet the demand for railway travel between Zhejiang's two largest cities, so construction of a new [high-speed railway line between Hangzhou and Ningbo](/wiki/Hangyong_Passenger_Railway "Hangyong Passenger Railway") started in 2009. The new railway line was finished in 2013 and reduced travel time between Ningbo and Hangzhou to 50 minutes.

The [Ningbo–Taizhou–Wenzhou Railway](/wiki/Ningbo%E2%80%93Taizhou%E2%80%93Wenzhou_Railway "Ningbo–Taizhou–Wenzhou Railway") is a high-speed railway that opened in September 2009. It connects Ningbo with cities along the coast to the south to [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") Province. High-speed trains on this line operate at speeds of up to 250 km/h (160 mph).

Ningbo re-opened the [Ningbo railway station](/wiki/Ningbo_railway_station "Ningbo railway station") after three years of construction on 28 December 2013. With a construction area of more than 120,000 m2,[[69]](#cite_note-70) it is one of the largest railway stations in China.

### Expressway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Expressway")]

Seven expressways connect Ningbo with its surrounding cities:

* The Hangyong expressway, built in the 1990s, connects Hangzhou and Ningbo, and is now part of Hangzhou Bay ring expressway (G9211).
* The Yongtaiwen expressway (G15), opened in 2000, connects Ningbo with Taizhou and Wenzhou.
* The Yongjin expressway (G1512) connects Ningbo and Jinhua.
* The Huyong expressway (G15) connects Ningbo and Shanghai via the Hangzhou Bay bridge.
* The [Yongzhou expressway](/wiki/G9211_Yongzhou_Expressway "G9211 Yongzhou Expressway") (G9211) via [Jintang Bridge](/wiki/Jintang_Bridge "Jintang Bridge").[[70]](#cite_note-71)
* The [G1504 Ningbo Ring Expressway](/wiki/G1504_Ningbo_Ring_Expressway "G1504 Ningbo Ring Expressway")
* The [G1523 Ningbo–Dongguan Expressway](/wiki/G1523_Ningbo%E2%80%93Dongguan_Expressway "G1523 Ningbo–Dongguan Expressway")

## Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Military")]

Ningbo serves as the headquarters of the [East Sea Fleet](/wiki/East_Sea_Fleet "East Sea Fleet") of the Chinese [People's Liberation Army Navy](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Navy "People's Liberation Army Navy").

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Language")]

Ningbo speech is a dialect of [Wu Chinese](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") that has preserved many aspects of ancient Chinese phonology. Its original wording mode can be found in classical reference books. After the [unequal treaty](/wiki/Unequal_treaty "Unequal treaty") port opening, western culture gradually permeated Ningbo. Thus, the prefix "[洋](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B4%8B#Chinese "wikt:洋") yang", meaning ocean or Western, before the nouns of imported goods is a special language phenomenon of Ningbo dialect.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

## Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Food")]

Ningbo is known for Ningbo [Tangyuan](/wiki/Tangyuan "Tangyuan"), small stuffed buns which are boiled. The stuffing is usually ground sesame mixed with sugar or pork fat. The stuffing is then wrapped with sticky rice powder. Ningbo is even more well known throughout China for its seafood. Seafood markets are abundant, carrying extensive varieties of fish, shellfish, snails, jellyfish and other invertebrates, and sea vegetables in all stages of preparation from "still swimming," to cleaned and ready to cook, to fully cooked.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Education")]

Ningbo has a comprehensive education system encompassing higher education, secondary and primary schooling, and international education. As of the end of 2020, the city had 1,896 schools of all types, enrolling approximately 1.418 million students.[[72]](#cite_note-:0-73)

The city is also recognized as one of the world’s top 100 cities for scientific research output, according to the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index").[[16]](#cite_note-:1-17) In 2020, Ningbo continued to expand its human capital base, attracting 1,372 additional doctoral degree holders (bringing the total to 9,265), 67,000 highly skilled workers (totaling 551,000), and 166,000 university graduates, representing a year-on-year increase of 20.5%. The city hosted 35 postdoctoral research stations and trained more than 327,000 skilled personnel during the year.

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Compulsory education in Ningbo follows the national system, covering ages 6 to 15 and consisting of six years of primary school and three years of junior secondary school. Both public and private schools operate across the city. Preparation for the [Gaokao](/wiki/Gaokao "Gaokao") (高考), the national university entrance examination taken at the end of senior high school, is optional.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

By the end of 2020, Ningbo had 86 regular high schools enrolling about 93,000 students, 35 vocational secondary schools with 69,000 students, 230 junior high schools with approximately 217,000 students, and 427 primary schools with 517,000 students. The city also hosted 838 full-time private primary and secondary schools (including kindergartens), enrolling around 258,000 students, accounting for 21.7% of all full-time primary and secondary students. Children of migrant workers constituted a significant share of enrollments, with about 288,000 attending schools in Ningbo.[[72]](#cite_note-:0-73)

### International education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ningbo&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: International education")]

Ningbo hosts several international and international-division schools authorized to deliver foreign curricula and enroll international students as alternatives to the Chinese national curriculum.

[Access International Academy Ningbo](/wiki/Access_International_Academy_Ningbo "Access International Academy Ningbo") (AIAN) and [Ningbo Zhicheng School International](/wiki/Ningbo_Zhicheng_School_International "Ningbo Zhicheng School International") (NZSI) offer the American AERO (American Education Reaches Out) curriculum and [College Board](/wiki/College_Board "College Board") [Advanced Placement](/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") programs. The [Ningbo International School](http://www.nbis.net.cn/about.asp?acataid=10) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20200805040511/http://www.nbis.net.cn/about.asp?acataid=10) 2020-08-05 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") (NBIS) delivers the Cambridge International Primary and Secondary Curricula, leading to IGCSE and A-Level qualifications. Huamao Multicultural Education Academy[[74]](#cite_note-75) is an [IB World School](/wiki/IB_World_School "IB World School"), offering the [IB Primary Years Programme](/wiki/IB_Primary_Years_Programme "IB Primary Years Programme") and the [International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme](/wiki/International_Baccalaureate_Diploma_Programme "International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme").
